_meta.authoringNotes: Classification per Pollock et al. 2014 British Athletics Muscle Injury Classification (PMID 24875299) and Mueller-Wohlfahrt Munich consensus 2013 (PMID 23080315). Epidemiology per Ekstrand 2011 UEFA injury study (PMID 21878440), Orchard AFL 2002. Nordic exercise per Petersen 2011 (PMID 21795512), Van der Horst 2015 (PMID 25794888). Return-to-play criteria per Askling H-test (PMID 19961815) and Heiderscheit 2010 J Orthop Sports Phys Ther.

h1Title: Hamstring Strain: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Hamstring strain is an acute musculotendinous injury of the posterior thigh, most often involving the biceps femoris during high-speed running. It is the single most common injury in elite football (soccer), accounting for 12-17% of all injuries and 17-23% of time lost to injury (UEFA Elite Club Injury Study). The injury typically occurs at the end of the swing phase as the hamstring is eccentrically loaded to decelerate the lower leg before foot strike. Recurrence rates are high — 12-22% in professional football — because rehabilitation often ends before strength and neuromuscular control are fully restored. Modern management uses the British Athletics or Munich muscle injury classifications, prescribes a stepwise progressive loading and Nordic hamstring exercise programme, and uses objective return-to-play criteria (pain-free Askling H-test, isokinetic strength symmetry within 10%, full sport-specific performance) rather than fixed time intervals. Eccentric strengthening with the Nordic hamstring exercise prevents 51-65% of new and recurrent hamstring injuries.

keyStats.prevalence: 12-17% of all injuries in elite football; injury incidence 0.81-1.35 per 1,000 player exposure hours (UEFA Elite Club Injury Study)
keyStats.demographics: Male:female ratio approximately 2:1 in elite sport; women face higher rates in some sports (sprinting, soccer)
keyStats.avgAge: Peak incidence 16-30 years among athletes; older age and prior injury raise risk
keyStats.globalCases: Tens of millions of recreational and professional sports participants affected each year

definition: Hamstring strain (ICD-10: S76.31 strain of the muscle, fascia and tendon of the posterior muscle group at thigh level) is an acute musculotendinous injury of the posterior thigh, involving one of the three hamstring muscles (biceps femoris long head, semitendinosus, semimembranosus) and most often the proximal myotendinous junction of biceps femoris. Two mechanisms predominate: high-speed running (Type I) at the terminal swing phase, when the hamstring is eccentrically active and at near-maximal length to decelerate the lower limb; and stretching-related injury (Type II) during slow eccentric loading at extreme range, such as kicks, dance, and gymnastic splits. Type I injuries usually involve biceps femoris and recover faster than Type II proximal semimembranosus injuries. The British Athletics Muscle Injury Classification (BAMIC) describes severity by grade (0-4) and anatomical site (a = myofascial, b = myotendinous junction, c = intratendinous), with c-grade injuries carrying the longest return-to-play times. The Munich consensus classification distinguishes functional muscle disorders (Types 1a, 1b: fatigue-related and delayed-onset muscle soreness; Types 2a, 2b: spine-related and muscle-related neuromuscular disorder) from structural muscle injuries (Types 3a, 3b: minor and moderate partial tears; Type 4: complete tear or avulsion). Management is led by sports medicine, with input from physiotherapy, strength and conditioning, and orthopaedics for proximal tendon avulsions.

typesClassification.description: Discomfort during or after activity without structural disruption on MRI. Recovery typically 1-3 days. Includes Munich Type 1 and Type 2 disorders.
Less than 10% cross-sectional area involvement and lesion length under 5 cm on MRI. Return to play 7-14 days.
10-50% cross-sectional area involvement and 5-15 cm lesion. Return to play 16-25 days for myofascial/myotendinous; longer for intratendinous (c) lesions.
Over 50% cross-sectional area involvement and lesion above 15 cm. Return to play 4-8 weeks or longer; intratendinous extension delays recovery further.

typesClassification.type: Grade 4 — complete tear or proximal tendon avulsion
typesClassification.description: Complete musculotendinous disruption or avulsion of the proximal common hamstring tendon from the ischial tuberosity. Surgical repair indicated in active patients to restore function and reduce chronic symptoms.

primarySymptoms: Sudden sharp pain in the posterior thigh during sprinting, kicking, or stretching, often described as feeling a 'pull' or 'pop'.
Inability to continue running at full speed; many athletes stop within one or two strides of injury.
Localised tenderness on palpation over the muscle belly, myotendinous junction, or proximal tendon, depending on the site of injury.
Pain on resisted knee flexion and hip extension with the knee straight.
Limited range of motion on passive straight-leg raise compared to the contralateral side.
Bruising on the back of the thigh developing 24-72 hours after the injury and tracking down to the popliteal fossa or calf.
Antalgic gait with a shortened stride length on the affected side.
Audible 'snap' or 'pop' at the moment of injury, more typical of complete tears and avulsions.
Sitting discomfort with deep pain at the ischial tuberosity in proximal tendon avulsion injuries.
earlyWarningSigns: Tightness or mild discomfort in the posterior thigh during or after high-speed running
Reduced eccentric hamstring strength on routine pre-season screening
Recurrent low-grade calf or lumbar pain in an athlete returning to sport
Persistent flexibility loss on active or passive straight-leg raise compared with the other side
Previous hamstring injury in the same leg within the past 12 months
emergencySigns: Sudden severe pain with audible pop and inability to bear weight — possible complete tendon rupture or avulsion fracture
Major bruising extending from the buttock to the knee or beyond within 24 hours — significant haematoma; consider compartment or arterial injury in severe trauma
Numbness, weakness, or foot drop after a posterior thigh injury — sciatic nerve compression by haematoma or scarring
Severe sitting pain and inability to lift the leg out straight after a fall — proximal hamstring avulsion warranting urgent imaging and surgical assessment
Open wound with muscle visible after laceration or compound injury — surgical emergency

causes.cause: High-speed running and sprinting (Type I injury)
causes.description: Most hamstring strains occur during the terminal swing phase of sprinting when the biceps femoris is eccentrically active at near-maximum length to decelerate the lower leg. Football, rugby, sprint athletics, cricket, and Australian rules football account for most cases.

causes.cause: Stretching, kicking, and slow eccentric extreme range loading (Type II injury)
causes.description: Dance, gymnastics, slide tackles, splits, and high kicks load the proximal semimembranosus near its limit of length, producing slow-onset tears at or near the ischial tuberosity. These injuries take longer to recover.

Eccentric hamstring strength deficits (low Nordic hamstring strength, low hamstring:quadriceps ratio under 0.6) predict injury risk. Athletes failing to maintain eccentric capacity during the competitive season are at higher risk.

causes.cause: Insufficient warm-up and high training load
causes.description: Cold muscle and acute load spikes in training (more than 1.5 times the chronic 4-week average) are associated with 2-3 fold higher hamstring injury risk in elite athletes.
Most match injuries occur in the final 15 minutes of each half, when fatigue impairs neuromuscular coordination. Limited recovery time between matches doubles injury risk.

A prior injury raises recurrence risk 2-6 fold, especially within the first 12 weeks of return to sport. Inadequate rehabilitation, residual strength deficits, and unresolved scarring underlie the recurrence rate.

Low back stiffness, poor pelvic control, reduced hip extension range, and quadriceps dominance increase hamstring loading and injury risk. Sacroiliac dysfunction and sciatic nerve tension can also contribute.

riskFactors.description: The single strongest predictor: prior injury raises recurrence risk 2-6 fold within 12 months. Reinjury rates of 12-22% are reported in professional football.
Each additional year of age above 25 raises hamstring strain risk by 1.3-1.8 fold in pooled cohorts of professional athletes.

riskFactors.factor: Sport with high-speed running or eccentric loading
riskFactors.description: Football, rugby, sprint athletics, Australian rules football, cricket, and dance carry incidence of 0.5-2 per 1,000 exposure hours; rates are lower in basketball and cycling.
Athletes in the lowest Nordic-hamstring strength quartile have 2-3 fold higher injury risk. Nordic hamstring exercise reduces injury by 51-65%.

riskFactors.factor: Reduced hamstring flexibility and short fascicle length
riskFactors.description: Short biceps femoris fascicle length (under 11 cm) measured by ultrasound is associated with injury. Eccentric training increases fascicle length.
Acute:chronic workload ratios above 1.5 carry 2-3 fold higher injury risk. Periodised training and adequate recovery reduce risk.

riskFactors.factor: Fatigue and inadequate recovery between matches
riskFactors.description: Two matches within 4 days double hamstring injury risk in elite football. Rotation, recovery strategies, and sleep optimisation are protective.
Low back stiffness, reduced hip extension, and pelvic control deficits raise hamstring injury risk; addressed in modern rehabilitation programmes.

affectedDemographics: Elite football (soccer): incidence 0.81-1.35 per 1,000 player exposure hours; 12-17% of all injuries (UEFA Elite Club Injury Study).
Track and field sprinters: hamstring injuries make up 24% of all injuries; 12-22% recurrence within 12 months.
Recreational runners: lower incidence than elite athletes but still 1-3% annual injury rate among regular runners.
Female athletes: relatively higher rate in some sports (collegiate American football, soccer) than males in similar sports.
Older athletes (over 25 years) and those with prior injury: significantly higher risk of incident and recurrent injury.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis is clinical, supplemented by imaging in elite athletes and severe injuries. History characterises the mechanism (high-speed running vs slow stretching), exact moment of injury, prior injury history, location of pain, and ability to continue activity. Examination assesses inspection (bruising, swelling, palpable defect), palpation along the hamstring muscles and ischial tuberosity, passive and active range of motion, straight-leg raise (compared with the other side), resisted knee flexion at 15 and 90 degrees, and the Askling H-test (active straight-leg raise to limit). The H-test is sensitive for residual pain and is a key return-to-play criterion. Functional tests include single-leg squat, single-leg hop, and sprint mechanics. Imaging is not required for grade 1 injuries when diagnosis is clear, but MRI is standard practice in elite athletes for grading injury, identifying intratendinous involvement, and informing prognosis. The British Athletics Muscle Injury Classification or Munich consensus classification is applied to MRI findings. Ultrasound has comparable sensitivity for muscle injuries but is operator-dependent. For suspected proximal tendon avulsion, MRI defines retraction distance (over 2 cm and complete two-tendon or three-tendon tear favour surgery). Plain radiograph excludes ischial avulsion fracture in adolescents and bony injuries in older patients with osteoporosis. Differential diagnoses include referred lumbar pain, sciatic nerve entrapment, posterior thigh compartment syndrome, deep vein thrombosis, and ischiogluteal bursitis.

diagnosticTests.test: Clinical examination with palpation and resisted knee flexion
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms diagnosis, localises injury (proximal vs distal, medial vs lateral), and grades severity
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Physiotherapist or sports medicine doctor examines for tenderness, swelling, gaps, and pain on resisted contraction. Takes 10-15 minutes.

diagnosticTests.test: Askling H-test (active straight-leg raise to pain limit)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Sensitive functional test for residual injury and return-to-play readiness
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Patient lies supine and lifts the straight leg as high as possible to the point of any discomfort. Compared with the uninjured side. Quick clinical test, 2-3 minutes.

diagnosticTests.test: MRI of the thigh (Spectral T2 / STIR sequences)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Grades injury severity (BAMIC, Munich), identifies intratendinous involvement, and predicts return-to-play time
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Patient lies in the MRI scanner for 20-30 minutes. No radiation. Report within 24-48 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects muscle disruption, haematoma, and tendon injury; useful for serial assessment and guiding rehabilitation
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Patient lies prone while the sonographer applies gel and scans the muscle. Takes 15-20 minutes. Operator dependent.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies hamstring strength and hamstring:quadriceps ratios; identifies eccentric strength deficits
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed at 60 and 240 degrees per second on a dynamometer. Takes 20-30 minutes; results compared to the uninjured side.

diagnosticTests.test: Plain radiograph of the pelvis and femur (selected cases)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes ischial avulsion fracture in adolescents and bony injuries in adults
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard pelvic and AP femoral views, 5-10 minutes.

treatmentOverview: Initial management follows the PEACE & LOVE acute care framework: Protect (avoid pain-aggravating activity for 1-3 days), Elevate, Avoid anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs may impair early muscle healing), Compression, and Education in the acute phase; followed by Load (early progressive loading), Optimism, Vascularisation (cardiovascular exercise that does not stress the injury), and Exercise to restore strength, range, and proprioception. Cryotherapy is used for symptomatic relief in the first 24-48 hours but does not change long-term outcomes. Paracetamol provides pain control without inhibiting muscle healing. Rehabilitation proceeds through stages: (1) reduce pain and oedema with relative rest and isometric exercises; (2) restore range of motion with gentle stretching and Nordic hamstring exercise eccentric work; (3) progressive resistance training emphasising eccentric strength, hip extension, and lumbopelvic control; (4) running progression starting with straight-line jogging, building to sprints and change-of-direction work; (5) sport-specific drills and return-to-play. Modern rehabilitation emphasises eccentric strengthening with the Nordic hamstring exercise, lengthened-state strength work (Romanian deadlifts, single-leg deadlifts), and trunk and pelvic control. Return-to-play decisions use objective criteria — pain-free Askling H-test, isokinetic strength symmetry within 10%, single-leg hop within 10%, full sport-specific drills without discomfort — rather than fixed time windows. Platelet-rich plasma injections have shown no clear benefit in randomised trials and are not routinely recommended. Surgery is reserved for complete proximal tendon avulsion (two- or three-tendon tear with more than 2 cm retraction) where early repair within 4-6 weeks gives superior strength and return-to-sport rates compared with conservative management.

medicalTreatments.name: PEACE & LOVE acute and subacute care framework
medicalTreatments.description: Protect, Elevate, Avoid NSAIDs, Compression, Education for first 1-3 days; then Load, Optimism, Vascularisation, Exercise from day 3 onwards. Replaces older RICE and PRICE acronyms.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces time to functional milestones in observational cohorts; aligns with current sports medicine consensus

medicalTreatments.name: Paracetamol (acetaminophen) 500-1,000 mg up to four times daily
medicalTreatments.description: Provides analgesia without inhibiting muscle protein synthesis or early repair. Combine with relative rest and early progressive loading.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Adequate analgesia for grade 1-2 injuries; allows early rehabilitation

medicalTreatments.name: Progressive eccentric hamstring strengthening (Nordic hamstring exercise)
medicalTreatments.description: Eccentric loading from 25 repetitions per week (3 sessions of 3-8 reps over 2 sets) in the early phase, progressing to higher volumes. Adapted to injury stage and tolerance.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces new and recurrent hamstring injuries by 51-65% (Petersen 2011; Van der Horst 2015 meta-analysis)

medicalTreatments.name: Lengthened-state hamstring strength work (Romanian deadlift, single-leg deadlift)
medicalTreatments.description: Strength training in lengthened muscle positions targets the most injury-prone region of the biceps femoris. 2-3 sessions weekly in the late rehabilitation phase.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Increases fascicle length and reduces re-injury risk in retrospective cohorts

medicalTreatments.name: Lumbopelvic stability training
medicalTreatments.description: Core, gluteal, and trunk control exercises addressing lumbopelvic dysfunction. Includes plank progressions, dead bugs, and single-leg work.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces hamstring injury recurrence in randomised trials when added to standard rehabilitation (Sherry 2004 J Orthop Sports Phys Ther)

medicalTreatments.name: Graded running and sprint progression
medicalTreatments.description: From straight-line jogging through tempo runs, accelerations, near-maximal sprints, change-of-direction work, and sport-specific drills. Progression guided by symptoms and objective testing.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Critical to safe return to sport; premature return is the dominant cause of re-injury

surgicalOptions.name: Open repair of complete proximal hamstring tendon avulsion
surgicalOptions.description: Open surgical repair through a posterior gluteal incision, with reattachment of the avulsed tendons to the ischial tuberosity using suture anchors. Recommended for complete two- or three-tendon tears with more than 2 cm of retraction, especially in active patients.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 85-95% return to pre-injury sport with early repair (within 4-6 weeks); 60-75% with delayed repair beyond 6 months

surgicalOptions.name: Surgical evacuation of large symptomatic haematoma
surgicalOptions.description: Rarely required. Reserved for large painful collections, infection, or compression of the sciatic nerve. Most haematomas resolve with conservative management.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Symptomatic improvement in 80-90% of selected patients

alternativeTreatments.name: Soft-tissue manual therapy (deep tissue, instrument-assisted)
alternativeTreatments.description: Used as an adjunct to exercise in the subacute and remodelling phases. Reduces pain, improves range of motion, and may help with scar maturation. Limited high-quality evidence for outcome benefit.

alternativeTreatments.name: Blood flow restriction training (BFR)
alternativeTreatments.description: Low-load resistance training with a pneumatic cuff occluding venous return. Builds muscle strength with lower mechanical loads, useful in early rehabilitation when traditional heavy lifting is not tolerated.

whySeeSpecialist: Most hamstring strains are managed by primary care, sports medicine, and physiotherapy. Specialist sports medicine or orthopaedic referral is needed for grade 3-4 injuries, suspected proximal tendon avulsion, persistent pain beyond 8 weeks, recurrent injuries, or athletes seeking rapid return to elite competition. Imaging-guided injection therapy is offered by some sports medicine clinics.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a sports medicine physician or musculoskeletal physiotherapist with experience in injury classification (BAMIC, Munich), MRI interpretation, and progressive eccentric rehabilitation. Look for clinics that use objective return-to-play criteria (Askling H-test, isokinetic strength, sport-specific drills) rather than time-only protocols.
hospitalCriteria: Sports medicine clinic with MRI access
Physiotherapy with strength and conditioning expertise
Orthopaedic surgery service for tendon repair
keyFacilities: Orthopaedic and trauma units for surgical repair
preventionStrategies: Perform Nordic hamstring exercises at least twice weekly in-season (3 sets of 5-8 reps).
Include lengthened-state strength work (Romanian deadlifts, single-leg deadlifts) twice weekly.
Warm up with at least 10-15 minutes of dynamic mobility, gradual sprint build-up, and sport-specific drills.
Manage training load with weekly monitoring; avoid acute:chronic workload ratios above 1.5.
Allow adequate recovery between matches (at least 72 hours between high-intensity exposures).
Address lumbopelvic dysfunction, hip mobility, and pelvic control proactively.
lifestyleModifications: Follow the prescribed rehabilitation programme — premature return to sport doubles re-injury risk.
Continue Nordic and lengthened-state hamstring exercises lifelong in athletes.
Hydrate adequately and maintain good sleep (7-9 hours) to support recovery.
Use foam rolling and dynamic stretching before, and static stretching after, sport.
Monitor early warning signs (tightness, asymmetric flexibility) and pause high-intensity training when they appear.
Maintain healthy body weight to reduce relative load on hamstrings during sprinting.

recommended: Adequate protein intake (1.6-2.2 g/kg/day in athletes) distributed across meals
Anti-inflammatory whole-food diet with fruits, vegetables, oily fish, and olive oil
Tart cherry juice and other polyphenol-rich foods which may reduce muscle soreness
Adequate hydration (35-40 mL/kg/day plus exercise replacement)
avoid: Excess alcohol which impairs muscle protein synthesis and recovery
Heavy NSAID use during early healing (may impair muscle regeneration)
Severely energy-restricted diets which slow tissue repair
Smoking, which reduces microvascular healing capacity

exerciseGuidelines: Resume range-of-motion exercises within 24-72 hours of injury. Add isometric and concentric strength work in week 1. Begin eccentric (Nordic hamstring) training when pain on resisted contraction has settled, typically week 2-3. Progress to running, sprinting, and change-of-direction work over weeks 3-8 depending on grade. Continue Nordic and lengthened-state work indefinitely as part of injury-prevention programmes.
dailyManagement: Perform prescribed rehabilitation exercises twice daily during the acute phase.
Apply ice for 15-20 minutes every 2-3 hours in the first 48 hours for symptom relief.
Use compression shorts during the early phase to reduce swelling.
Avoid NSAIDs in the first 3-5 days where possible.
Use paracetamol for analgesia and to allow rehabilitation exercises.
Keep a training and symptom diary to track progress through return-to-play stages.
prognosis: Most grade 1 injuries return to sport within 7-14 days and grade 2 injuries within 16-25 days. Grade 3 injuries take 4-8 weeks, longer when intratendinous involvement is present. Surgically repaired proximal tendon avulsions return to sport in 4-6 months with 85-95% achieving pre-injury performance when repaired within 4-6 weeks. Recurrence rates remain high — 12-22% in professional football — and are halved by structured eccentric strengthening and objective return-to-play criteria. Long-term complications include chronic tendinopathy of the proximal hamstring tendons (especially after repeated injuries or surgical repair), reduced sprint performance compared with the contralateral leg in elite athletes, and ischiogluteal bursitis. Earlier return to sport using only time-based criteria is the strongest predictor of re-injury. Older athletes (over 25 years) and those with prior injury face the highest recurrence risk.
recoveryTimeline: Grade 1: 7-14 days return to play. Grade 2: 16-25 days for myofascial/myotendinous injuries; 28-40 days for intratendinous (BAMIC 'c') injuries. Grade 3: 4-8 weeks. Grade 4 surgical: 4-6 months. Nordic hamstring exercise should continue indefinitely as in-season prevention.
complications: Hamstring re-injury within 12 months (12-22% in professional football) — minimised by eccentric strength training and objective return-to-play criteria.
Chronic proximal hamstring tendinopathy with deep buttock pain — addressed by eccentric loading, shockwave therapy, and occasional injection.
Sciatic nerve entrapment in mature scar tissue or post-surgical fibrosis — rare but disabling.
Persistent strength deficit in the injured leg compared with the contralateral side, affecting sprint performance.
Ischiogluteal bursitis from chronic proximal tendon overload.
Heterotopic ossification or myositis ossificans within the muscle, particularly after large haematomas (rare).

supportResources.name: British Journal of Sports Medicine — Hamstring Injury Resources
supportResources.description: Leading sports medicine journal with open-access hamstring injury reviews and consensus statements.

supportResources.name: American Medical Society for Sports Medicine (AMSSM)
supportResources.description: US sports medicine specialty society with patient and clinician resources.

supportResources.name: FIFA 11+ injury-prevention programme
supportResources.description: Globally adopted warm-up and exercise programme that reduces hamstring and other injuries in football.

supportResources.name: British Athletics Muscle Injury Classification resources
supportResources.description: Original BAMIC classification paper and educational materials for clinicians and athletes.

confusedWithConditions.name: Back Pain (sciatica)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Sciatic nerve referral from the lumbar spine causes posterior thigh pain that often extends below the knee and is reproduced by lumbar movement or straight-leg raise without local hamstring tenderness. Hamstring strain has local tenderness, pain on resisted knee flexion, and a clear sport-related mechanism.

confusedWithConditions.name: Chronic Low Back Pain
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Chronic low back pain dominates in the lumbar spine and gluteal region with limited posterior thigh involvement. Hamstring strain is acute, sport-related, and centred on the muscle belly or proximal tendon.

confusedWithConditions.name: Muscle Contusion
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Contusion follows a direct blow (e.g., knee or boot impact) rather than a high-speed running mechanism. Pain and bruising are typically over the quadriceps or anterior thigh; haematoma is more central.

confusedWithConditions.name: Sarcopenia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Sarcopenia is age-related muscle loss in older adults, not an acute injury. Hamstring strain is an acute event in younger athletes; sarcopenia produces chronic weakness and falls in older adults.

relatedConditions.name: Muscle Contusion
relatedConditions.relevance: Another common acute muscle injury in sport; differs in mechanism and treatment but shares rehabilitation principles.

relatedConditions.name: Back Pain
relatedConditions.relevance: Lumbopelvic dysfunction and sciatic referral are differentials and contributors to recurrent hamstring injury.

relatedConditions.name: Chronic Low Back Pain
relatedConditions.relevance: Lumbar pathology and pelvic control deficits predispose athletes to hamstring injury.

relatedConditions.name: Rheumatoid Arthritis
relatedConditions.relevance: Inflammatory arthritis can rarely cause posterior thigh pain through enthesitis at the ischial tuberosity, an important differential in older patients.

faqs.question: What is a hamstring strain?
faqs.answer: A hamstring strain is an acute injury of the back-of-thigh muscles (biceps femoris, semitendinosus, semimembranosus) where muscle fibres tear during high-speed running, kicking, or stretching. It usually presents with sudden pain, inability to continue sport, and tenderness on palpation.

faqs.question: How long does it take to recover from a hamstring strain?
faqs.answer: Grade 1 (minor) injuries recover in 7-14 days. Grade 2 (moderate) injuries take 16-25 days for muscle-belly injuries and longer for tendon-involved injuries. Grade 3 injuries take 4-8 weeks. Complete tendon avulsions require surgery and 4-6 months rehabilitation.

faqs.question: What are the signs of a hamstring strain?
faqs.answer: Common signs include sudden sharp pain during sprinting or kicking, bruising on the back of the thigh, tenderness on palpation, pain on resisted knee flexion, limited straight-leg raise, and difficulty walking or running. Some athletes hear a pop or feel a tear.

faqs.question: Do I need an MRI for a hamstring strain?
faqs.answer: Minor injuries with clear clinical diagnosis do not always need imaging. MRI is recommended in elite athletes, suspected grade 2-3 injuries, recurrent injuries, or when intratendinous involvement is suspected because it grades severity and predicts return-to-play time.

faqs.question: Should I use ice for a hamstring injury?
faqs.answer: Ice for 15-20 minutes every 2-3 hours in the first 24-48 hours reduces pain and swelling but does not improve long-term outcomes. Modern guidelines emphasise early progressive loading after the very acute phase rather than prolonged rest and icing.

faqs.question: Can I take ibuprofen for a hamstring strain?
faqs.answer: Avoid NSAIDs in the first 3-5 days if possible because they may impair muscle regeneration. Paracetamol is preferred for early pain control. Short-course NSAIDs can be used later if needed, especially with severe pain.

faqs.question: What exercises help hamstring recovery?
faqs.answer: Progressive rehabilitation includes isometric, then concentric, then eccentric (Nordic hamstring) exercises, lengthened-state strength work (Romanian deadlifts), lumbopelvic stability work, and graded running and sprint progression. A physiotherapist should guide the programme.

faqs.question: What is the Nordic hamstring exercise?
faqs.answer: The Nordic hamstring exercise is a partner-assisted or device-supported eccentric strengthening exercise in which the athlete kneels and lowers the torso toward the floor under control. Performed regularly, it reduces new and recurrent hamstring injuries by 51-65%.

faqs.question: When can I return to sport?
faqs.answer: Return when pain-free on Askling H-test, isokinetic strength symmetry within 10%, full single-leg hop within 10% of the other side, and full sport-specific drills without symptoms. Time-based return alone has high re-injury rates.

faqs.question: How common are hamstring injuries?
faqs.answer: Hamstring strains are the most common injury in elite football, accounting for 12-17% of all injuries and 17-23% of time lost. Annual incidence is 0.81-1.35 per 1,000 player exposure hours. They are also common in sprinting, rugby, Australian rules football, and dance.

faqs.question: Why do hamstring injuries keep coming back?
faqs.answer: Recurrence (12-22% in football) reflects premature return to sport, residual strength deficits, inadequate eccentric loading, unresolved scarring, and lumbopelvic dysfunction. Structured eccentric rehabilitation and objective return-to-play criteria reduce recurrence substantially.

faqs.question: Does PRP help hamstring injuries?
faqs.answer: Randomised trials of platelet-rich plasma in acute hamstring injuries have shown no consistent benefit over standard rehabilitation, and most current consensus statements do not recommend PRP for routine use. Selected chronic proximal tendinopathy cases may be considered.

faqs.question: Do I need surgery for a hamstring strain?
faqs.answer: Most hamstring injuries do not need surgery. Surgical repair is reserved for complete proximal tendon avulsion (two- or three-tendon tear with more than 2 cm retraction) where early repair within 4-6 weeks gives the best long-term outcomes.

faqs.question: Can I prevent hamstring injuries?
faqs.answer: Yes, partly. Nordic hamstring exercises, lengthened-state strength work, lumbopelvic stability training, adequate warm-up, and careful training load management reduce hamstring injuries by 50% or more in randomised trials.

faqs.question: What is the difference between grade 1 and grade 2?
faqs.answer: Grade 1 injuries have small (under 10% cross-sectional area) tears with mild pain and minimal strength loss; recovery 7-14 days. Grade 2 injuries have moderate (10-50%) tears with significant pain, bruising, and weakness; recovery 16-25 days or longer with tendon involvement.

faqs.question: Should I stretch a hamstring injury?
faqs.answer: Aggressive stretching is avoided in the first 1-2 weeks; gentle pain-free range-of-motion exercises start within 24-72 hours. Lengthened-state strength training matters more than static stretching for long-term injury prevention.

faqs.question: Can older adults get hamstring strains?
faqs.answer: Yes. Recreational and master athletes regularly sustain hamstring injuries; age over 25 raises risk 1.3-1.8 fold per year. Recovery may be slower in older athletes and Nordic hamstring exercise remains protective at all ages.

faqs.question: What is a proximal hamstring avulsion?
faqs.answer: Proximal hamstring avulsion is a complete tear of the tendons from the ischial tuberosity (sitting bone), often during waterskiing, falls, or major sports trauma. It causes immediate buttock pain, bruising tracking down the thigh, sitting pain, and inability to lift the straight leg.

faqs.question: Can I run with a hamstring strain?
faqs.answer: Avoid running until pain at rest and on walking has settled, usually 3-7 days for grade 1 injuries and longer for grade 2-3. Premature running doubles re-injury risk. Follow a graded progression from straight-line jogging through tempo runs to sprints.

faqs.question: Do compression shorts help?
faqs.answer: Compression shorts may reduce swelling, support the muscle during early healing, and provide proprioceptive feedback. Evidence for performance benefit is mixed, but most athletes tolerate them well during return to sport.

faqs.question: When should I see a doctor?
faqs.answer: See a clinician for severe pain, inability to bear weight, large bruising, audible pop, numbness or weakness, or any suspected complete tear or avulsion. Recurrent injuries and elite-level performance also warrant sports medicine assessment for imaging and a structured return-to-play plan.

regionalNames.name: ハムストリング肉離れ

metaTitle: Hamstring Strain: Symptoms, Causes & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: Hamstring strain: pain in the back of thigh from running or kicking. Grades, Nordic exercise, MRI, surgery for avulsion, and return-to-play criteria.

sources.title: Pollock N, James SL, Lee JC, Chakraverty R. 2014. British athletics muscle injury classification: a new grading system. British Journal of Sports Medicine 48(18):1347-1351.

Mueller-Wohlfahrt HW, Haensel L, Mithoefer K, et al. 2013. Terminology and classification of muscle injuries in sport: the Munich consensus statement. British Journal of Sports Medicine 47(6):342-350.

Petersen J, Thorborg K, Nielsen MB, Budtz-Jorgensen E, Holmich P. 2011. Preventive effect of eccentric training on acute hamstring injuries in men's soccer. American Journal of Sports Medicine 39(11):2296-2303.

van der Horst N, Smits DW, Petersen J, Goedhart EA, Backx FJ. 2015. The preventive effect of the Nordic hamstring exercise on hamstring injuries in amateur soccer players. American Journal of Sports Medicine 43(6):1316-1323.
Ekstrand J, Hagglund M, Walden M. 2011. Epidemiology of muscle injuries in professional football (soccer). American Journal of Sports Medicine 39(6):1226-1232.

Askling CM, Tengvar M, Saartok T, Thorstensson A. 2007. Acute first-time hamstring strains during high-speed running: a longitudinal study including clinical and MRI findings. American Journal of Sports Medicine 35(2):197-206.

Heiderscheit BC, Sherry MA, Silder A, Chumanov ES, Thelen DG. 2010. Hamstring strain injuries: recommendations for diagnosis, rehabilitation, and injury prevention. Journal of Orthopaedic & Sports Physical Therapy 40(2):67-81.
